title: Touchables and Buttons
description: Handle user interactions with TouchableOpacity, Button, and other touchable components.

slides.title: Touchable Components 👆
slides.content:
# Making Things Interactive

React Native provides several **touchable** components that respond to user presses. They all accept an `onPress` prop.

## Common Touchables

* **`TouchableOpacity`** – fades the content when pressed (most common).
* **`TouchableHighlight`** – darkens the background when pressed.
* **`TouchableWithoutFeedback`** – responds to presses but gives no visual feedback.
* **`TouchableNativeFeedback`** (Android only) – uses Android's native ripple effect.

Also, there's a simple **`Button`** component for basic needs.

slides.title: TouchableOpacity Example
slides.content: Wrap any component with TouchableOpacity to make it tappable:
slides.codeSnippet:
import { TouchableOpacity, Text } from 'react-native';

<TouchableOpacity onPress={() => alert('Pressed!')}>
  <Text>Tap me</Text>
</TouchableOpacity>

slides.title: The Button Component 🎛️
slides.content:
## Simple Button

If you just need a basic button with a title, use the `Button` component:

```jsx
import { Button } from 'react-native';

<Button
  title="Press me"
  onPress={() => console.log('Pressed')}
  color="#841584" // optional
/>
```

`Button` is limited in styling – it always looks like the platform's default button. For custom styling, use a Touchable component.

quizData.question: Which Touchable component is most commonly used because it gives a nice opacity feedback?
quizData.explanation: TouchableOpacity fades the content, providing a smooth visual feedback that is widely used.

slides.title: Other Touchable Props 🎁
slides.content:
## Useful Props

* `disabled` – if true, disables the touchable.
* `onLongPress` – called when the press is held for a while.
* `delayPressIn` / `delayPressOut` – delays in milliseconds.
* `hitSlop` – extends the touchable area without affecting layout.

Example with `hitSlop`:
```jsx
<TouchableOpacity
  onPress={handlePress}
  hitSlop={{ top: 10, bottom: 10, left: 10, right: 10 }}
>
  <Text>Harder to miss</Text>
</TouchableOpacity>
```

slides.title: Custom Styled Button
slides.content: Combine TouchableOpacity with View and Text to create a fully custom button:
slides.codeSnippet:
<TouchableOpacity
  onPress={handlePress}
  style={{
    backgroundColor: 'blue',
    padding: 12,
    borderRadius: 8,
    alignItems: 'center',
  }}
>
  <Text style={{ color: 'white', fontSize: 16 }}>Custom Button</Text>
</TouchableOpacity>

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: Use `TouchableOpacity` for most cases. It's simple and looks great. For Android, you can add `TouchableNativeFeedback` to get the material ripple effect, but be sure to wrap it in a condition for platform compatibility.
